title: Galaxies and Large-Scale Structure of the Universe

blocks.text: A galaxy is a gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, gas, dust, and dark matter. The Universe contains an estimated 200 billion to 2 trillion galaxies, each containing millions to trillions of stars. Galaxies are the fundamental building blocks of the Universe's large-scale structure, arranged in clusters, superclusters, and filaments surrounding vast empty voids.

Edwin Hubble (1926) classified galaxies into three main morphological types: (1) Spiral galaxies — disc-shaped with spiral arms, ongoing star formation, contain young and old stars (e.g., Milky Way, Andromeda). (2) Elliptical galaxies — smooth, featureless ellipsoidal shapes, predominantly old red stars, minimal gas/dust, little new star formation. (3) Irregular galaxies — lack defined structure, often chaotic, vigorous star formation (e.g., Large and Small Magellanic Clouds).

The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 100,000 light-years in diameter, containing 200-400 billion stars. It has a central bar-shaped structure surrounded by spiral arms. Our Solar System is located in the Orion Arm (also called the Local Spur), about 26,000 light-years from the galactic centre. The Sun orbits the galactic centre once every 225-250 million years (one 'galactic year').

At the centre of the Milky Way lies Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*), a supermassive black hole with a mass of approximately 4 million solar masses. Most large galaxies harbour supermassive black holes at their centres. These central black holes influence galactic evolution through energy feedback when actively accreting matter (Active Galactic Nuclei).

The Local Group is a galaxy cluster containing about 80 galaxies dominated by the Milky Way and Andromeda (M31). The Milky Way and Andromeda are approaching each other at ~110 km/s and will merge in approximately 4.5 billion years. The Local Group is part of the Virgo Supercluster, which itself is part of the Laniakea Supercluster — a vast cosmic structure containing approximately 100,000 galaxies spanning 500 million light-years.

The large-scale structure of the Universe resembles a cosmic web: galaxies cluster along filaments and walls surrounding enormous voids (regions almost empty of galaxies). The largest known structures include the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall (~10 billion light-years across) and the Boötes Void (~330 million light-years in diameter). This web-like distribution arose from tiny density fluctuations in the early Universe amplified by gravity over billions of years.

Understanding galaxies contextualises Earth's position in the cosmos: we occupy an unremarkable location in an average spiral arm of a typical barred spiral galaxy in a modest galaxy group. This 'Copernican Principle' — that there is nothing special about our cosmic address — is a foundational concept in modern cosmology and has philosophical implications for understanding our place in the Universe.

NCERT Class 6 — 'The Earth: Our Habitat', Chapter 1: States 'A Galaxy is a huge system of billions of stars and clouds of dust and gases.' NCERT mentions that there are millions of galaxies and that our galaxy is the Milky Way. While basic, UPSC has framed questions testing whether students know the Sun is one star among billions in one galaxy among billions.

NCERT Class 11 — Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 2: Provides context by stating the Universe contains billions of galaxies. The emphasis is on scale: Universe → Galaxies → Solar Systems → Planets. This hierarchical organisation establishes the framework within which Earth's formation is understood.

NCERT Class 8 — Science, 'Stars and the Solar System': Describes the Milky Way as appearing as a bright band across the night sky when viewed edge-on from within. Mentions that the Sun is just one of the many stars in our galaxy, establishing the Sun's ordinary nature — critical for understanding that planet-forming conditions are likely common across the galaxy.

sections.title: Standard Reference Depth (Savindra Singh / GC Leong)

blocks.text: GC Leong provides the Hubble classification (tuning fork diagram): Elliptical (E0-E7, numbered by elongation), Spiral (Sa, Sb, Sc — tightness of arms), Barred Spiral (SBa, SBb, SBc), and Irregular (Irr). The Milky Way was reclassified from spiral to BARRED spiral (SBbc) in 2005 based on infrared surveys revealing the central bar structure.

Savindra Singh discusses the Milky Way's structural components: (1) Central bulge — dense, old stars, supermassive black hole. (2) Disc — spiral arms, young stars, gas, dust, active star formation. (3) Halo — globular clusters, old stars, dark matter. (4) Dark matter halo — extends far beyond visible disc, contains most of the galaxy's mass. The disc is where our Solar System resides.

Distance measurement in astronomy uses a ladder of methods: Parallax (nearby stars, <100 pc), Cepheid Variables (galaxies, period-luminosity relation discovered by Henrietta Leavitt), Type Ia Supernovae (distant galaxies, 'standard candles'), and Redshift (Hubble's Law, v = H₀d, for the most distant objects). Each method calibrates the next, building from local to cosmological distances.

Hubble's Law (1929): Galaxies recede from us at velocities proportional to their distance (v = H₀d, where H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc). This is evidence for universal expansion. Key insight: it appears as if we are at the centre, but expansion is uniform — every point sees all others receding. There is no centre to the expansion, just as there is no centre to the surface of an expanding balloon.

sections.title: Current Affairs Connected to This Topic

blocks.text: The Event Horizon Telescope released the first image of Sagittarius A* (May 2022) — the supermassive black hole at the centre of our Milky Way. The image shows the 'shadow' of the black hole surrounded by a bright ring of hot gas. This confirmed the mass (~4 million solar masses) and confirmed general relativity predictions for our own galactic centre.

JWST has discovered galaxies existing just 300-400 million years after the Big Bang — much earlier than expected. Some of these early galaxies are unexpectedly massive and mature, challenging existing models of galaxy formation. This is an active area of research that may require revisions to our understanding of early Universe structure formation.

The Euclid space telescope (ESA, launched July 2023) is mapping the large-scale structure of the Universe to study dark energy and dark matter. It will survey 36% of the sky, imaging billions of galaxies up to 10 billion light-years away. Understanding cosmic structure helps constrain the nature of dark energy driving accelerated expansion.

India's AstroSat continues contributing to galaxy research. In 2024, it detected extreme ultraviolet radiation from a distant galaxy, demonstrating that such radiation can escape galaxies and ionise intergalactic hydrogen — relevant to understanding how the early Universe became transparent (cosmic reionisation). India's observation capabilities are reaching internationally competitive levels.

TRAP 1 — 'The Sun is at the centre of the Milky Way.' WRONG. The Sun is approximately 26,000 light-years from the galactic centre, located in the Orion Arm. At the centre is the supermassive black hole Sagittarius A*. This is a frequently tested incorrect statement in UPSC Prelims.

TRAP 2 — 'The Milky Way is the largest galaxy.' WRONG. The Milky Way is an average-sized galaxy. Andromeda (M31) is larger in our Local Group. IC 1101, one of the largest known galaxies, is about 6 million light-years in diameter — 60 times the Milky Way's size.

TRAP 3 — Confusing 'galaxy' with 'universe.' The Universe contains all galaxies. A galaxy is one system of stars within the Universe. The observable Universe contains approximately 2 trillion galaxies. UPSC sometimes presents statements conflating these scales in incorrect options.

TRAP 4 — 'Andromeda is moving away from us because the Universe is expanding.' WRONG. Andromeda is actually APPROACHING the Milky Way at ~110 km/s. Expansion applies to the large-scale Universe, but locally, gravitational attraction can overcome expansion. Galaxies within clusters are gravitationally bound and do not participate in Hubble expansion.

TRAP 5 — Light-year confusion. A light-year is a unit of DISTANCE, not time. 1 light-year ≈ 9.46 trillion kilometres. When we observe a galaxy 10 billion light-years away, we see it as it was 10 billion years ago — we are looking back in time. This dual nature (distance and lookback time) is a powerful observational tool.

Q1 (2017): Consider the following: 1. The Sun is approximately at the centre of the Milky Way galaxy. 2. The Sun takes approximately 250 million years to orbit the galactic centre. Which is/are correct? (a) 1 only (b) 2 only (c) Both (d) Neither

Q2 (2021): With reference to the structure of the Universe, consider the following: 1. Galaxies are grouped into clusters. 2. Clusters are grouped into superclusters. 3. The Milky Way belongs to the Virgo Supercluster. Which are correct? (a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2, and 3
Q3 (2022): A 'light-year' is a unit of: (a) Time (b) Speed (c) Distance (d) Intensity of light
Q1 (GS-1, 2017, 10 marks): 'Describe the structure of the Milky Way galaxy and the position of our Solar System within it.'

Q2 (GS-3, 2022, 15 marks): 'How has modern space-based astronomy expanded our understanding of the Universe? Discuss with reference to recent discoveries.' [Approach: Include JWST, EHT, gravitational waves, AstroSat — show expanding observational frontier]

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q1: Consider the following: 1. The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy. 2. The nearest large galaxy to the Milky Way is the Andromeda galaxy. 3. The Andromeda galaxy is moving away from the Milky Way due to cosmic expansion. Which are correct? (a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2, and 3

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q2: Hubble's Law states that: (a) All galaxies rotate at the same rate (b) The velocity of recession of a galaxy is proportional to its distance (c) Larger galaxies contain more dark matter (d) The Universe has a defined centre from which all galaxies expand

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q3: At the centre of the Milky Way galaxy is: (a) A massive star cluster called Sagittarius (b) A supermassive black hole called Sagittarius A* (c) A dense nebula producing new stars (d) A quasar emitting radio waves
MAINS PRACTICE — Q1 (150 words): 'Explain Hubble's Law and its significance in understanding the expanding Universe.'

MAINS PRACTICE — Q2 (250 words): 'Describe the hierarchical organisation of the Universe from planetary systems to superclusters. How does this understanding inform our perspective on Earth's cosmic significance?'

PRELIMS PYQ ANSWERS: Q1 (2017): Answer (b) — Sun is NOT at centre (it's 26,000 ly away in Orion Arm). Galactic year of ~250 million years is correct. Q2 (2021): Answer (d) — All three are correct. Galaxies → clusters → superclusters is the hierarchy, and Milky Way is in Virgo Supercluster (part of Laniakea). Q3 (2022): Answer (c) — A light-year is a unit of distance (≈9.46 trillion km).

PRACTICE ANSWERS: Q1: Answer (a) — Milky Way IS a barred spiral and Andromeda IS the nearest large galaxy. But Andromeda is moving TOWARDS us (not away), so Statement 3 is wrong. Q2: Answer (b) — Hubble's Law: v = H₀d, recession velocity proportional to distance. There is no centre to expansion. Q3: Answer (b) — Sagittarius A* is the supermassive black hole (~4 million solar masses) at the Milky Way's centre, imaged by EHT in 2022.

KEY FACTS FOR PRELIMS: (1) Sun is 26,000 ly from galactic centre in Orion Arm. (2) Milky Way is barred spiral, ~100,000 ly diameter. (3) Andromeda approaching us, not receding. (4) Light-year = distance, not time. (5) Hubble's Law: v = H₀d. (6) Observable universe: ~2 trillion galaxies. (7) Sgr A* = supermassive black hole at galactic centre.

KEY FOCUS FOR MAINS: (1) Hierarchical structure — planets → stars → galaxies → clusters → superclusters → cosmic web. (2) Modern observational breakthroughs (EHT, JWST, gravitational waves). (3) India's contributions (AstroSat, LIGO-India). (4) Philosophical dimension — Copernican Principle and Earth's unremarkable cosmic position for Essay papers.
